segments.title: Oil, territory, and global naval reach
segments.summary: The campaign map shows why access to oil and dispersed territories shape where fleets can operate and expand.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the campaign map to explain his attention to oil-producing regions and distant bases. The game turns possession of places such as Persia and Pearl Harbor into practical naval reach, rather than treating territorial expansion as an abstract score. It is a useful introduction to how fuel access and geography constrain a global fleet.

evidence.note: The campaign recap connects oil, controlled territory, Pearl Harbor, and future invasions.

segments.title: Fast battlecruisers as a hunting force
segments.summary: A group of 32-knot battlecruisers is organized to find and overwhelm a weaker enemy force while destroyers screen it.
segments.body: The campaign fleet is built around speed: the battlecruisers are detached to hunt while smaller ships take scouting and screening roles. Dr. Clarke contrasts that task organization with the much slower opposing cruiser. The moment makes clear why speed can turn a nominal engagement into a pursuit the weaker side cannot escape.

evidence.note: He describes battlecruisers built to battlecruiser standards, their 32-knot speed, and assigns hunting, scouting, and screening roles.

segments.title: Speed and concentration against isolated cruisers
segments.summary: A concentrated battlecruiser-and-destroyer force illustrates how badly isolated cruisers fare when outnumbered and outrun.
segments.body: The game encounter emphasizes force concentration rather than a balanced duel. Six battlecruisers and a destroyer screen close on a cruiser, while the speed margin prevents the target from choosing the terms of contact. It is a watch point on the tactical value of pairing heavy, fast ships with smaller escorts.

evidence.note: The opposing cruiser faces six battlecruisers, another battlecruiser, and destroyers; Dr. Clarke compares the odds to roughly twenty to one.

segments.title: Forming a battle line for accurate gunfire
segments.summary: The engagement demonstrates how a battle line, controlled speed, and a broadside create a decisive gunnery advantage.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke brings his battle line in abreast and slows it once close enough to improve accuracy. He calls out the combined broadside of thirteen-and-a-half-inch, six-inch, and smaller guns, then explains the target's predicament as a drawn-out pincer. The watch point connects formation, speed control, and arcs of fire in a way that a ship-stat screen cannot.

evidence.note: He orders a line abreast, slows for accuracy, describes the broadside, and identifies the resulting pincer.

segments.title: Building a home-defence fleet and submarine mass
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke shifts ships toward home defence while explaining the value of a large submarine force in reserve.
segments.body: After declining a risky battle, he redistributes battlecruisers to create a force for home defence. The fleet plan is backed by eighty newly available submarines, with further construction deferred until the next technological improvement. The passage shows the campaign balancing immediate readiness, reserves, and modernization.

evidence.note: He assembles a home-defence fleet and reports eighty E-class submarines in service.

segments.title: Why refit older capital ships?
segments.summary: Older battlecruisers and battleships remain useful when refits preserve the tonnage needed for battles and invasions.
segments.body: Rather than discard older hulls automatically, Dr. Clarke keeps and refits ships that can still contribute to an operation. He argues that a fleet may need raw tonnage to deploy for an invasion or a battle, even when those older ships are not its most valuable units. Improved guns and selective upgrades make them more capable without demanding entirely new construction.

evidence.note: He scraps obsolete small craft but retains older battlecruisers and explains refits as a way to provide needed operational tonnage.

segments.title: Combining armour, speed, and heavy guns
segments.summary: Two campaign designs explore the trade-offs and attractions of high speed, heavy armour, and exceptionally large broadsides.
segments.body: The King Edward VII design combines 16.5-inch guns, a 17.3-inch belt, and 30 knots, illustrating the appeal of a fast, heavily protected capital ship. A second design pursues an even larger broadside and all-round twelve-inch belt protection. The discussion is useful for seeing how the game makes the familiar triangle of firepower, protection, and speed feel financially and technically consequential.

evidence.note: He details the guns, belt armour, speed, and intended shock effect of two new capital-ship designs.

segments.title: Torpedo threats and destroyer screening
segments.summary: A close action shows why screens matter: destroyers make torpedo runs while fast capital ships exploit damaged arcs and speed.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke checks whether his screen is actually between the heavy ship and danger, then notes that the formation is used for destroyer torpedo runs. His battlecruiser maneuvers toward the enemy's weaker side rather than seeking a symmetrical exchange. The action also reveals a limitation of his own force: poor torpedo technology weakens destroyer effectiveness even when the tactical opportunity exists.

evidence.note: The battle discusses screening, enemy torpedo attacks, firing on the weaker side, and inadequate torpedo technology.

segments.title: Searching through fog with battlecruisers
segments.summary: Six fast battlecruisers adapt their formation and speed while searching for an enemy concealed by fog.
segments.body: In poor visibility, Dr. Clarke tries to bring fast battlecruisers into a useful line-abreast formation, then adjusts speed so lagging ships can catch up. The result is an improvised staggered formation rather than the intended textbook line. It is a compact example of how visibility, formation keeping, and scouting are inseparable tactical problems.

evidence.note: He sets six 32-knot battlecruisers line abreast in fog, slows them to reform, and searches for the hidden enemy.

segments.title: Why all-big-gun ships still need smaller guns
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains that rapid-fire secondary guns remain necessary to counter destroyers and torpedo boats.
segments.body: The game prompt leads to a useful corrective about the all-big-gun battleship. Even Dreadnought retained smaller weapons, Dr. Clarke notes, because destroyers and torpedo boats demanded a faster-firing answer. The segment is a concise reminder that a capital ship's main battery does not replace close-range defence.

evidence.note: He cites Dreadnought's smaller guns as protection against destroyers and torpedo boats.

segments.title: Concentrating forces before a northern Japan campaign
segments.summary: The campaign shifts to the Indian Ocean and northern Japan, where dispersed fleets must be concentrated before conquest is practical.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies Japanese colonies and northern Japan as possible objectives, then searches the map for fleets that can be repositioned. He moves capital ships, destroyers, and submarines while acknowledging that not every theatre has as many forces as hoped. By the time 640,000 tons are assembled, the point is clear: distant operations depend on assembling enough combat power before seeking decisive action.

evidence.note: He plans operations against Japanese possessions, gathers ships from multiple regions, and reports concentrating 640,000 tons.
